title: Eleven Lights — One Shore Remains
journal:
The Shiva Purana speaks of twelve Jyotirlingas — places where the infinite pillar of light is remembered in stone and ritual. I have stood in eleven of those courtyards. Each visit felt less like collecting stamps and more like recalibrating pace: queues at dawn, cold floors, the same mantra in different accents of India.

Completed (11):
1. Somnath — Veraval, Gujarat
2. Mallikarjuna — Srisailam, Andhra Pradesh
3. Mahakaleshwar — Ujjain, Madhya Pradesh
4. Omkareshwar — Khandwa, Madhya Pradesh
5. Baidyanath (Vaidyanath) — Deoghar, Jharkhand
6. Bhimashankar — Pune region, Maharashtra
7. Nageshwar — Dwarka, Gujarat
8. Kashi Vishwanath (Vishweshwar) — Varanasi, Uttar Pradesh
9. Trimbakeshwar — Nashik, Maharashtra
10. Kedarnath — Uttarakhand
11. Grishneshwar — near Ellora, Maharashtra

Still ahead (1):
12. Ramanathaswamy — Rameswaram, Tamil Nadu — the southernmost of the twelve, tied to the Sethu and to patience.

The geography changes; the vow stays simple. Show up. Walk in. Leave lighter. The yatra is not finished until that Rameswaram corridor is walked. That unfinished mile is intentional.
memories: Bhimashankar · Nageshwar · Kashi Vishwanath · Trimbakeshwar · Kedarnath · Grishneshwar

title: Kedarnath
journal:
Kedarnath sits in the Garhwal Himalaya as both Jyotirlinga and high pilgrimage — snow lines, Mandakini roar, and a stone temple that only opens for part of the year. The trek from Gaurikund is a long conversation with altitude: slow steps, shared tea, the decision to keep moving when lungs argue.

Above the noise of the trail, the shrine feels abrupt — grey stone against white peaks. It is not a casual weekend; it is a reminder that some systems (and some journeys) only run in season, and that preparation is respect.
memories: Temple season windows — nature’s deploy schedule
Silence that outlasts the crowd noise

title: Kalavantin Durga
journal:
Kalavantin is a Sahyadri pinnacle near Prabalgad — roughly 686 m, famous for rock-cut steps that go near-vertical at the end. From Thakurwadi the path is honest: forest, Prabalmachi plateau, then the exposed climb where balance matters more than speed.

No romance about railings — there aren’t many. You earn the summit view of the Western Ghats one careful foothold at a time. It is the kind of trek that teaches focus the way production incidents do: stay present, or you slip.
memories: 60° rock steps and a clear head
Prabalmachi as the calm before the pinnacle
Respect for height — optional fear, required attention

title: Rajgad
journal:
Rajgad — “king of forts” — was capital ground for Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj for years. The massif rises around 1,376 m with machis (Padmavati, Suvela, Sanjeevani) and the Ballekilla crown. From Gunjavane the approach mixes forest trail and stone, then opens into ruins that feel like architecture designed for weather and war.

Walking Rajgad is walking history with tired calves. The scale forces humility: empires needed high ground; engineers still do.
memories: Fort sprawl that refuses a single photo

title: Garbett Plateau
journal:
Garbett Plateau sits in the Matheran hills — a wide green table after a Sahyadri climb, especially alive in monsoon mist. The trail trades technical rock for endurance and mud: longer rhythm, cooler air, views that arrive suddenly when clouds tear open.

It is less about a summit spike and more about arriving somewhere flat enough to breathe and look sideways at the ranges. Plateau walks recalibrate speed.
memories: A summit that feels like a living room of grass
Mist as a feature, not a bug

title: Gorakhgad
journal:
Gorakhgad is a compact Sahyadri pin with rock-cut steps and caves tied to Nath tradition. The climb is short relative to Himalayan days, but the chimneys and holds demand the same attention as Kalavantin’s final metres.

From the top, the Murbad landscape spreads quietly. These “small” Maharashtra forts are where weekend courage is practiced — so bigger days feel earned, not borrowed.

title: Sagargad
journal:
Sagargad rises above the Alibag side of the Konkan — fort remnants, sea-facing air, and a trek that blends coastal humidity with Sahyadri stone. Compared with Himalayan approaches, it feels intimate: shorter logistics, still enough elevation to clear a work week.

From the ridge you sense both forest and coastline. It is a reminder that adventure does not always require a flight — sometimes only a early start and respect for the hill.
memories: Sea breeze on a fort trail
